title: SSH private keys should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.pattern: -----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----[\s\S]+?-----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----
checks.message: SSH RSA private key detected - CRITICAL. This RSA private key provides authentication access to SSH servers, Unix/Linux systems, Git repositories, and network devices. Exposed SSH keys enable attackers to: 1) Gain unauthorized remote access to servers with the privileges of the key owner (potentially root/administrator), 2) Access Git repositories and commit malicious code with identity impersonation, 3) Pivot to other systems using SSH agent forwarding or jump hosts, 4) Exfiltrate sensitive data from compromised systems, 5) Install backdoors, malware, or cryptocurrency miners, 6) Establish persistent access through authorized_keys modifications, 7) Access cloud infrastructure (AWS EC2, Azure VMs, GCP Compute), 8) Compromise CI/CD pipelines and deployment systems. IMMEDIATE ACTIONS: 1) Remove the exposed private key from source code, configuration files, and version control history immediately (use git-filter-branch, BFG Repo-Cleaner, or git-filter-repo), 2) Revoke/delete the compromised key from all authorized_keys files on target servers, 3) Generate new SSH key pair with strong passphrase protection (ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096), 4) Audit SSH access logs (/var/log/auth.log, /var/log/secure) for unauthorized access attempts or successful logins, 5) Review command history (.bash_history, .zsh_history) on accessed systems for malicious activity, 6) Check for unauthorized modifications to authorized_keys, .ssh/config, and cron jobs, 7) Implement SSH key management policies: use SSH certificates with short validity periods, require passphrase protection, store keys in hardware tokens (YubiKey), or use SSH CA, 8) Enable SSH key monitoring and alerting for new key additions, 9) Review and restrict SSH access using firewall rules, fail2ban, or IP allowlisting, 10) Implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) for SSH where possible, 11) Rotate all potentially compromised credentials on affected systems, 12) Conduct forensic analysis if unauthorized access is suspected.

checks.pattern: -----BEGIN OPENSSH PRIVATE KEY-----[\s\S]+?-----END OPENSSH PRIVATE KEY-----
checks.message: SSH OpenSSH private key detected - CRITICAL. This OpenSSH format private key (modern SSH key format introduced in OpenSSH 7.8+) provides authentication access to SSH servers and can support multiple key types (RSA, ECDSA, Ed25519). Exposed OpenSSH keys enable attackers to: 1) Authenticate to SSH servers without password, gaining shell access with user privileges, 2) Access cloud instances (AWS EC2, Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine) and container orchestration platforms (Kubernetes, Docker), 3) Clone and modify Git repositories hosted on GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket with full commit access, 4) Access CI/CD systems (Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI) to inject malicious build steps, 5) Traverse network infrastructure through SSH tunneling and port forwarding, 6) Compromise deployment pipelines and production environments, 7) Execute lateral movement to additional systems via SSH agent forwarding. IMMEDIATE ACTIONS: 1) Remove exposed OpenSSH private key from all locations (source code, configs, version control), 2) Purge from Git history using git-filter-repo, BFG Repo-Cleaner, or git-filter-branch, 3) Delete compromised public key from authorized_keys files on all target servers (~/.ssh/authorized_keys, /root/.ssh/authorized_keys), 4) Generate new SSH key pair with strong encryption (recommendations: ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -a 100 for Ed25519, or ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 for RSA), 5) Use passphrase protection on new private key (ssh-keygen will prompt), 6) Review SSH server logs for unauthorized access (/var/log/auth.log on Debian/Ubuntu, /var/log/secure on RHEL/CentOS), 7) Check for signs of compromise: unauthorized processes, network connections (netstat, ss), modified system files, new user accounts, 8) Audit and remove any unauthorized entries in authorized_keys, .ssh/config, known_hosts, 9) Implement SSH best practices: disable password authentication (PasswordAuthentication no), use SSH certificates, implement key rotation policies, 10) Enable SSH access monitoring and alerting, 11) Consider using hardware security keys (YubiKey with PIV) or SSH CA for centralized key management, 12) Restrict SSH access by IP address using firewall rules or TCP wrappers (/etc/hosts.allow, /etc/hosts.deny).

checks.pattern: -----BEGIN EC PRIVATE KEY-----[\s\S]+?-----END EC PRIVATE KEY-----
checks.message: SSH EC (Elliptic Curve) private key detected - CRITICAL. This Elliptic Curve private key provides SSH authentication with strong security and performance (ECDSA P-256, P-384, P-521 curves). Exposed EC SSH keys enable attackers to gain the same unauthorized access as traditional RSA keys but are often used in high-security environments due to their cryptographic strength. EC keys are commonly used for: SSH server authentication, Git repository access (GitHub/GitLab deploy keys), cloud infrastructure access (AWS, Azure, GCP), container orchestration (Kubernetes), and IoT/embedded device authentication. IMMEDIATE ACTIONS: 1) Remove exposed EC private key immediately from all storage locations, 2) Purge from Git repository history using appropriate tools (git-filter-repo recommended), 3) Revoke public key from authorized_keys on all SSH servers, 4) Generate new EC key pair with appropriate curve (recommended: ssh-keygen -t ecdsa -b 521 for P-521 curve, or consider Ed25519 for better performance and security), 5) Apply passphrase protection to new private key, 6) Audit SSH access logs for unauthorized connection attempts or successful authentications, 7) Review systems accessed with compromised key for signs of breach (unusual processes, network connections, file modifications), 8) Check for persistence mechanisms: cron jobs, startup scripts, backdoor accounts, rootkits, 9) Rotate all credentials and secrets on potentially compromised systems, 10) Implement SSH hardening: disable root login (PermitRootLogin no), use SSH certificates, enable SSH key expiration, 11) Deploy SSH session recording and monitoring solutions, 12) Consider migrating to Ed25519 keys (ssh-keygen -t ed25519) for superior security and performance if infrastructure supports it.

SSH private key detected (PKCS#8 format) - CRITICAL. This PKCS#8 format private key is a generic encrypted private key container that can hold RSA, EC, or Ed25519 keys used for SSH authentication. This modern format (replacing traditional PEM formats) is the default in newer OpenSSH versions and provides better security through standardized encryption. Exposure grants attackers full SSH access capabilities. IMMEDIATE ACTIONS: 1) Remove key from all locations immediately, 2) Purge from version control history, 3) Revoke from all authorized_keys files, 4) Generate new key pair with strong passphrase, 5) Audit access logs for unauthorized usage, 6) Check compromised systems for backdoors, unauthorized accounts, or malicious modifications, 7) Rotate all related credentials and secrets, 8) Implement SSH key lifecycle management: regular rotation, expiration policies, centralized key storage, 9) Enable SSH logging and monitoring, 10) Consider SSH certificates with short validity periods instead of long-lived keys.
